nodes.title: Production Deployment and System Integration
nodes.description: Deploy the AI learning system to production environments. Integrate all components into a unified platform and ensure system reliability.
learning_points: Deploy AI learning applications with proper infrastructure
Integrate multiple AI components into a cohesive learning platform
Implement monitoring, scaling, and maintenance procedures

sub_nodes.title: Production Deployment Strategies
sub_nodes.description: Moving AI learning systems from development to production.
learning_points: Containerized deployment with Kubernetes for scalability
Model serving infrastructure: batch and real-time inference
Load balancing and caching strategies for AI applications
Cost optimization for LLM API usage and model hosting

details.detailed_content:
## Understanding Production Deployment for AI Systems

### Why Production Deployment Matters

When you develop an AI-assisted learning system in a notebook or local environment, you have full control over resources and can tolerate slow response times. Production deployment introduces new challenges: variable traffic patterns, the need for high availability, security requirements, monitoring, and cost management. A learning system that works perfectly for one user may fail catastrophically when serving 1,000 concurrent learners.

### Containerized Deployment with Kubernetes

Containerization packages your application along with all its dependencies, ensuring consistent behavior regardless of the deployment environment. Docker containers isolate your AI application from system-level conflicts and enable reproducible deployments.

Kubernetes, the industry standard for container orchestration, provides essential production capabilities:

**Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA)** automatically adjusts the number of container instances based on CPU utilization, memory usage, or custom metrics. For AI applications, you might scale based on request queue length or inference latency rather than simple CPU metrics.

**Rolling Updates and Rollbacks** allow you to deploy new model versions without downtime. Kubernetes gradually replaces old pods with new ones while maintaining service availability.

**Self-Healing** restarts failed containers, replaces unresponsive pods, and reschedules work to healthy nodes automatically.

**Namespaces and Resource Quotas** help you isolate different environments (development, staging, production) and prevent any single team or application from consuming all cluster resources.

### Model Serving Infrastructure

Model serving addresses how your trained models are exposed to applications. There are two primary paradigms:

**Real-Time Inference (Online Serving)** processes individual requests as they arrive, returning predictions within milliseconds to seconds. This approach suits interactive learning applications where students expect immediate feedback. Real-time serving typically uses REST or gRPC APIs, with models loaded into memory on specialized inference servers like TensorFlow Serving, TorchServe, or Triton Inference Server.

**Batch Inference (Offline Serving)** processes large volumes of requests accumulated over time. This approach suits periodic tasks like grading assignments overnight, generating personalized content bundles, or running analytics. Batch jobs can be scheduled with tools like Apache Airflow or Kubernetes CronJobs.

A sophisticated production system often implements both patterns: real-time inference for interactive features and batch processing for intensive computations that don't require immediate results.

### Load Balancing Strategies

Load balancers distribute incoming requests across multiple model instances, preventing any single instance from becoming overwhelmed. For AI applications, simple round-robin distribution may not be optimal because inference requests can have vastly different computational requirements.

**Intelligent Routing** considers current load, instance health, and request characteristics. For example, requests for simpler models might be routed differently than complex multi-model pipelines.

**Connection Pooling** maintains persistent connections to backend services, reducing the overhead of establishing new connections for each request.

**Health Checks** continuously verify that model instances are responsive and producing valid outputs, not just that they're running.

### Caching Strategies for AI Applications

Caching in AI systems differs from traditional web caching because identical inputs may not produce identical outputs (especially with stochastic models), and inference results can be computationally expensive to generate.

**Semantic Caching** stores inference results based on semantic similarity rather than exact input matching. For learning systems, this means caching responses for similar questions, allowing students who ask about the same concept to receive cached results.

**Prompt Caching** is particularly valuable for LLM-based applications. When a base prompt remains constant and only the student-specific input changes, you can cache the computed prompt embeddings and reuse them.

**Embedding Cache** stores vector representations of frequently accessed content (course materials, questions, previous student inputs), enabling fast similarity searches without recomputing embeddings.

### Cost Optimization for LLM and Model Hosting

LLM API costs can quickly dominate your operational budget. Effective cost optimization requires multiple strategies:

**Model Selection** matches model capability to task complexity. Not every request needs GPT-4 class capabilities; simpler tasks may work well with smaller, faster, cheaper models.

**Prompt Compression** reduces token count without losing essential information, directly reducing API costs.

**Response Caching** prevents redundant API calls for identical or semantically similar requests.

**Batch Processing** combines multiple requests into single API calls where the provider supports it, reducing per-request overhead.

**Self-Hosting Consideration** evaluates whether hosting open-source models on your infrastructure makes financial sense compared to API usage, considering your volume, latency requirements, and data privacy needs.

**Reserved Capacity** offers significant discounts compared to on-demand pricing for predictable workloads, such as core learning system features used during business hours.

## Code Examples

### Example 1

```text
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ai-learning-service
  labels:
    app: ai-learning
    environment: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ai-learning
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ai-learning
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: inference-server
        image: your-registry/ai-learning:v2.1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "2Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
          limits:
            memory: "4Gi"
            cpu: "2000m"
        env:
        - name: MODEL_PATH
          value: "/models/tutor-model"
        - name: MAX_BATCH_SIZE
          value: "16"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5
        volumeMounts:
        - name: model-storage
          mountPath: /models
      volumes:
      - name: model-storage
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: model-pvc
```

### Example 2

```text
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: ai-learning-hpa
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: ai-learning-service
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80
  - type: Pods
    pods:
      metric:
        name: inference_queue_depth
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: "10"
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 10
        periodSeconds: 60
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 100
        periodSeconds: 15
      - type: Pods
        value: 4
        periodSeconds: 15
      selectPolicy: Max
```

### Example 3

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List, Optional
import asyncio
import hashlib
import time
from functools import lru_cache

app = FastAPI(title="AI Learning Inference Server")

# Simple in-memory cache for demonstration
# In production, use Redis with semantic similarity matching
inference_cache = {}
request_queue = asyncio.Queue()

class InferenceRequest(BaseModel):
    student_id: str
    question: str
    context: Optional[List[str]] = []
    model_name: str = "default"

class InferenceResponse(BaseModel):
    answer: str
    confidence: float
    model_used: str
    cached: bool
    latency_ms: float

async def process_inference_batch(requests: List[InferenceRequest]) -> List[dict]:
    # Simulate batch inference processing
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # Actual model inference would happen here
    results = []
    for req in requests:
        cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{req.question}:{req.model_name}".encode()).hexdigest()
        if cache_key in inference_cache:
            cached_result = inference_cache[cache_key]
            cached_result["cached"] = True
            results.append(cached_result)
        else:
            result = {
                "answer": f"Processed response for: {req.question[:50]}",
                "confidence": 0.92,
                "model_used": req.model_name,
                "cached": False
            }
            inference_cache[cache_key] = result
            results.append(result)
    return results

@app.post("/v1/inference", response_model=InferenceResponse)
async def inference(request: InferenceRequest):
    start_time = time.time()
    
    cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{request.question}:{request.model_name}".encode()).hexdigest()
    
    if cache_key in inference_cache:
        result = inference_cache[cache_key].copy()
        result["cached"] = True
        result["latency_ms"] = (time.time() - start_time) * 1000
        return result
    
    # Add to queue for batch processing
    await request_queue.put(request)
    
    # Process in batch if queue is large enough
    batch = []
    if request_queue.qsize() >= 5:
        for _ in range(min(5, request_queue.qsize())):
            batch.append(await request_queue.get())
        results = await process_inference_batch(batch)
        for r in results:
            inference_cache[cache_key] = r
    else:
        # Process single request
        results = await process_inference_batch([request])
        result = results[0]
        inference_cache[cache_key] = result
    
    result["latency_ms"] = (time.time() - start_time) * 1000
    return result

@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {"status": "healthy", "queue_depth": request_queue.qsize()}

@app.get("/metrics")
async def metrics():
    return {
        "cache_size": len(inference_cache),
        "queue_depth": request_queue.qsize(),
        "cache_hit_rate": 0.75
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
```

### Example 4

```python
import hashlib
import time
from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Any
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
import asyncio

class ModelTier(Enum):
    FAST_BUDGET = "fast_budget"      # Small, fast, cheap models
    BALANCED = "balanced"            # Mid-tier models
    PREMIUM = "premium"             # High-capability, expensive models

@dataclass
class LLMConfig:
    model_name: str
    tier: ModelTier
    cost_per_1k_tokens: float
    avg_latency_ms: float
    max_tokens: int

class CostOptimizer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.model_configs = {
            "gpt-4": LLMConfig("gpt-4", ModelTier.PREMIUM, 0.03, 5000, 8192),
            "gpt-3.5-turbo": LLMConfig("gpt-3.5-turbo", ModelTier.BALANCED, 0.002, 1000, 4096),
            "llama-2-7b": LLMConfig("llama-2-7b", ModelTier.FAST_BUDGET, 0.0, 200, 4096),
        }
        self.response_cache: Dict[str, Any] = {}
        self.usage_stats = {model: {"requests": 0, "tokens": 0, "cost": 0.0} 
                            for model in self.model_configs.keys()}
        self.daily_budget = 100.0
        self.today_spend = 0.0
    
    def estimate_cost(self, model: str, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int) -> float:
        config = self.model_configs.get(model)
        if not config:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown model: {model}")
        total_tokens = prompt_tokens + completion_tokens
        return (total_tokens / 1000) * config.cost_per_1k_tokens
    
    def select_model(self, task_complexity: str, urgency: str) -> str:
        # Model selection logic based on task requirements
        if self.today_spend >= self.daily_budget:
            return "llama-2-7b"  # Fallback to free model
        
        if urgency == "high" or task_complexity == "high":
            return "gpt-4"
        elif task_complexity == "medium":
            return "gpt-3.5-turbo"
        else:
            return "llama-2-7b"
    
    def get_cache_key(self, prompt: str, model: str) -> str:
        return hashlib.sha256(f"{prompt}:{model}".encode()).hexdigest()
    
    async def cached_llm_call(
        self, 
        prompt: str, 
        task_complexity: str = "medium",
        urgency: str = "normal",
        use_cache: bool = True
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        # Select appropriate model
        model = self.select_model(task_complexity, urgency)
        cache_key = self.get_cache_key(prompt, model)
        
        # Check cache first
        if use_cache and cache_key in self.response_cache:
            return {
                "response": self.response_cache[cache_key],
                "model": model,
                "cached": True,
                "cost": 0.0
            }
        
        # Estimate tokens (in production, use proper tokenizer)
        prompt_tokens = len(prompt.split()) * 1.3  # Rough approximation
        
        # Simulate LLM API call
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # Actual API call would happen here
        
        response = f"Generated response from {model} for: {prompt[:50]}..."
        completion_tokens = len(response.split())
        
        # Calculate and track cost
        cost = self.estimate_cost(model, int(prompt_tokens), completion_tokens)
        self.today_spend += cost
        
        self.usage_stats[model]["requests"] += 1
        self.usage_stats[model]["tokens"] += int(prompt_tokens + completion_tokens)
        self.usage_stats[model]["cost"] += cost
        
        # Cache the response
        if use_cache:
            self.response_cache[cache_key] = response
        
        return {
            "response": response,
            "model": model,
            "cached": False,
            "cost": cost,
            "total_spend_today": self.today_spend
        }
    
    def get_optimization_report(self) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        total_cost = sum(stats["cost"] for stats in self.usage_stats.values())
        total_requests = sum(stats["requests"] for stats in self.usage_stats.values())
        cache_hits = len(self.response_cache)
        
        return {
            "daily_budget": self.daily_budget,
            "today_spend": self.today_spend,
            "remaining_budget": self.daily_budget - self.today_spend,
            "total_requests": total_requests,
            "cache_size": cache_hits,
            "model_breakdown": self.usage_stats,
            "avg_cost_per_request": total_cost / total_requests if total_requests > 0 else 0
        }

# Usage example
async def main():
    optimizer = CostOptimizer()
    
    # Simple question - uses fast/cheap model
    result1 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="What is the capital of France?",
        task_complexity="low"
    )
    print(f"Simple question: {result1['model']}, Cost: ${result1['cost']:.4f}")
    
    # Complex question - uses premium model
    result2 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="Explain quantum entanglement and its implications for cryptography",
        task_complexity="high",
        urgency="high"
    )
    print(f"Complex question: {result2['model']}, Cost: ${result2['cost']:.4f}")
    
    # Same simple question - should hit cache
    result3 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="What is the capital of France?",
        task_complexity="low"
    )
    print(f"Cached question: {result3['model']}, Cached: {result3['cached']}, Cost: ${result3['cost']:.4f}")
    
    print("\nOptimization Report:")
    print(optimizer.get_optimization_report())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
```

```sql
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Copy Python packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /root/.local /home/appuser/.local

# Copy application code
COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup . .

    PATH=/home/appuser/.local/bin:$PATH

# Switch to non-root user
USER appuser

# Expose ports

# Health check
```

## Common Mistakes

- Deploying models without resource limits, causing Kubernetes pods to consume unlimited memory and crash nodes
- Using identical scaling policies for different model types; large language models require different thresholds than lightweight classifiers
- Neglecting cold start latency in serverless or auto-scaled deployments; first requests after scale-out can timeout
- Caching LLM responses without semantic similarity matching; exact-match caching misses opportunities where semantically similar inputs could share cached results
- Failing to implement circuit breakers for external LLM APIs; a provider outage cascades to your entire application
- Setting auto-scaling metrics based only on CPU/memory without considering inference queue depth, leading to poor responsiveness under load
- Not implementing request timeouts for model inference, allowing slow requests to block resources indefinitely
- Deploying without proper health checks for model readiness, routing traffic to pods still loading models
- Ignoring request payload size limits; large context windows can quickly exhaust memory on inference servers
- Skipping cost monitoring during development, leading to unexpected bills when production traffic patterns emerge


## Best Practices

- Use multi-stage Docker builds to minimize image size and attack surface; production images should contain only runtime dependencies
- Implement separate Kubernetes deployments for different model tiers (CPU inference, GPU inference) with appropriate resource requests
- Configure rolling deployments with maxSurge=1 and maxUnavailable=0 for zero-downtime updates of AI services
- Implement circuit breakers and fallback mechanisms for external API calls with graceful degradation to cached or simpler responses
- Use Prometheus metrics and Grafana dashboards specifically for AI metrics: inference latency percentiles, queue depth, cache hit rates, and cost tracking
- Enable request tracing (Jaeger, Zipkin) to identify bottlenecks across multi-model pipelines
- Implement token budgeting and request validation at the API gateway level before requests reach expensive inference resources
- Use model versioning with canary deployments: route 5-10% of traffic to new model versions before full rollout
- Configure appropriate PodDisruptionBudgets to ensure minimum replicas remain available during node maintenance
- Store model artifacts in shared PVCs or dedicated model registries (MLflow, Weights & Biases) rather than embedding in container images

from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List, Optional
import asyncio
import hashlib
import time
from functools import lru_cache

app = FastAPI(title="AI Learning Inference Server")

# Simple in-memory cache for demonstration
# In production, use Redis with semantic similarity matching
inference_cache = {}
request_queue = asyncio.Queue()

class InferenceRequest(BaseModel):
    student_id: str
    question: str
    context: Optional[List[str]] = []
    model_name: str = "default"

class InferenceResponse(BaseModel):
    answer: str
    confidence: float
    model_used: str
    cached: bool
    latency_ms: float

async def process_inference_batch(requests: List[InferenceRequest]) -> List[dict]:
    # Simulate batch inference processing
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # Actual model inference would happen here
    results = []
    for req in requests:
        cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{req.question}:{req.model_name}".encode()).hexdigest()
        if cache_key in inference_cache:
            cached_result = inference_cache[cache_key]
            cached_result["cached"] = True
            results.append(cached_result)
        else:
            result = {
                "answer": f"Processed response for: {req.question[:50]}",
                "confidence": 0.92,
                "model_used": req.model_name,
                "cached": False
            }
            inference_cache[cache_key] = result
            results.append(result)
    return results

@app.post("/v1/inference", response_model=InferenceResponse)
async def inference(request: InferenceRequest):
    start_time = time.time()
    
    cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{request.question}:{request.model_name}".encode()).hexdigest()
    
    if cache_key in inference_cache:
        result = inference_cache[cache_key].copy()
        result["cached"] = True
        result["latency_ms"] = (time.time() - start_time) * 1000
        return result
    
    # Add to queue for batch processing
    await request_queue.put(request)
    
    # Process in batch if queue is large enough
    batch = []
    if request_queue.qsize() >= 5:
        for _ in range(min(5, request_queue.qsize())):
            batch.append(await request_queue.get())
        results = await process_inference_batch(batch)
        for r in results:
            inference_cache[cache_key] = r
    else:
        # Process single request
        results = await process_inference_batch([request])
        result = results[0]
        inference_cache[cache_key] = result
    
    result["latency_ms"] = (time.time() - start_time) * 1000
    return result

@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {"status": "healthy", "queue_depth": request_queue.qsize()}

@app.get("/metrics")
async def metrics():
    return {
        "cache_size": len(inference_cache),
        "queue_depth": request_queue.qsize(),
        "cache_hit_rate": 0.75
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)

import hashlib
import time
from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Any
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
import asyncio

class ModelTier(Enum):
    FAST_BUDGET = "fast_budget"      # Small, fast, cheap models
    BALANCED = "balanced"            # Mid-tier models
    PREMIUM = "premium"             # High-capability, expensive models

@dataclass
class LLMConfig:
    model_name: str
    tier: ModelTier
    cost_per_1k_tokens: float
    avg_latency_ms: float
    max_tokens: int

class CostOptimizer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.model_configs = {
            "gpt-4": LLMConfig("gpt-4", ModelTier.PREMIUM, 0.03, 5000, 8192),
            "gpt-3.5-turbo": LLMConfig("gpt-3.5-turbo", ModelTier.BALANCED, 0.002, 1000, 4096),
            "llama-2-7b": LLMConfig("llama-2-7b", ModelTier.FAST_BUDGET, 0.0, 200, 4096),
        }
        self.response_cache: Dict[str, Any] = {}
        self.usage_stats = {model: {"requests": 0, "tokens": 0, "cost": 0.0} 
                            for model in self.model_configs.keys()}
        self.daily_budget = 100.0
        self.today_spend = 0.0
    
    def estimate_cost(self, model: str, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int) -> float:
        config = self.model_configs.get(model)
        if not config:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown model: {model}")
        total_tokens = prompt_tokens + completion_tokens
        return (total_tokens / 1000) * config.cost_per_1k_tokens
    
    def select_model(self, task_complexity: str, urgency: str) -> str:
        # Model selection logic based on task requirements
        if self.today_spend >= self.daily_budget:
            return "llama-2-7b"  # Fallback to free model
        
        if urgency == "high" or task_complexity == "high":
            return "gpt-4"
        elif task_complexity == "medium":
            return "gpt-3.5-turbo"
        else:
            return "llama-2-7b"
    
    def get_cache_key(self, prompt: str, model: str) -> str:
        return hashlib.sha256(f"{prompt}:{model}".encode()).hexdigest()
    
    async def cached_llm_call(
        self, 
        prompt: str, 
        task_complexity: str = "medium",
        urgency: str = "normal",
        use_cache: bool = True
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        # Select appropriate model
        model = self.select_model(task_complexity, urgency)
        cache_key = self.get_cache_key(prompt, model)
        
        # Check cache first
        if use_cache and cache_key in self.response_cache:
            return {
                "response": self.response_cache[cache_key],
                "model": model,
                "cached": True,
                "cost": 0.0
            }
        
        # Estimate tokens (in production, use proper tokenizer)
        prompt_tokens = len(prompt.split()) * 1.3  # Rough approximation
        
        # Simulate LLM API call
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # Actual API call would happen here
        
        response = f"Generated response from {model} for: {prompt[:50]}..."
        completion_tokens = len(response.split())
        
        # Calculate and track cost
        cost = self.estimate_cost(model, int(prompt_tokens), completion_tokens)
        self.today_spend += cost
        
        self.usage_stats[model]["requests"] += 1
        self.usage_stats[model]["tokens"] += int(prompt_tokens + completion_tokens)
        self.usage_stats[model]["cost"] += cost
        
        # Cache the response
        if use_cache:
            self.response_cache[cache_key] = response
        
        return {
            "response": response,
            "model": model,
            "cached": False,
            "cost": cost,
            "total_spend_today": self.today_spend
        }
    
    def get_optimization_report(self) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        total_cost = sum(stats["cost"] for stats in self.usage_stats.values())
        total_requests = sum(stats["requests"] for stats in self.usage_stats.values())
        cache_hits = len(self.response_cache)
        
        return {
            "daily_budget": self.daily_budget,
            "today_spend": self.today_spend,
            "remaining_budget": self.daily_budget - self.today_spend,
            "total_requests": total_requests,
            "cache_size": cache_hits,
            "model_breakdown": self.usage_stats,
            "avg_cost_per_request": total_cost / total_requests if total_requests > 0 else 0
        }

# Usage example
async def main():
    optimizer = CostOptimizer()
    
    # Simple question - uses fast/cheap model
    result1 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="What is the capital of France?",
        task_complexity="low"
    )
    print(f"Simple question: {result1['model']}, Cost: ${result1['cost']:.4f}")
    
    # Complex question - uses premium model
    result2 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="Explain quantum entanglement and its implications for cryptography",
        task_complexity="high",
        urgency="high"
    )
    print(f"Complex question: {result2['model']}, Cost: ${result2['cost']:.4f}")
    
    # Same simple question - should hit cache
    result3 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="What is the capital of France?",
        task_complexity="low"
    )
    print(f"Cached question: {result3['model']}, Cached: {result3['cached']}, Cost: ${result3['cost']:.4f}")
    
    print("\nOptimization Report:")
    print(optimizer.get_optimization_report())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

# RUN python -c "from transformers import AutoModel; AutoModel.from_pretrained('tutor-model')"

# Set environment variables
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PATH=/home/appuser/.local/bin:$PATH

# Switch to non-root user
USER appuser

# Expose ports
EXPOSE 8000 50051

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=40s --retries=3 \
    CMD curl -f http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1

# Run the application
CMD ["python", "-m", "uvicorn", "model_server:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

structured_content.title: Production Deployment Strategies
structured_content.summary: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to design and implement production-ready deployment pipelines for AI-assisted learning systems using containerization, model serving patterns, and cost optimization techniques.
structured_content.concept: Production deployment of AI-assisted learning systems transforms experimental models and algorithms into reliable, scalable services that can handle real-world usage patterns. Unlike development environments where data volumes are predictable and resources are abundant, production systems must handle variable load, ensure low latency responses, maintain data privacy, and optimize operational costs. The deployment strategy you choose directly impacts system reliability, user experience, and infrastructure expenses. A well-designed deployment strategy enables your AI learning system to serve hundreds or thousands of concurrent users while maintaining response quality and keeping costs under control. This is particularly critical for LLM-powered applications where API costs can escalate rapidly and response latency significantly affects user satisfaction.
steps: Containerize your AI application and model artifacts using Docker for consistent environments across development and production
Set up Kubernetes orchestration for automatic scaling, load balancing, and self-healing capabilities
Implement model serving infrastructure choosing between batch processing and real-time inference based on use case requirements
Configure caching layers and load balancing strategies to optimize response times and reduce redundant computations
Apply cost optimization techniques for LLM API usage and model hosting to control operational expenses

code_examples.description: Kubernetes deployment configuration for an AI learning service with resource limits and health checks
code_examples.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ai-learning-service
  labels:
    app: ai-learning
    environment: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ai-learning
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ai-learning
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: inference-server
        image: your-registry/ai-learning:v2.1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "2Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
          limits:
            memory: "4Gi"
            cpu: "2000m"
        env:
        - name: MODEL_PATH
          value: "/models/tutor-model"
        - name: MAX_BATCH_SIZE
          value: "16"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5
        volumeMounts:
        - name: model-storage
          mountPath: /models
      volumes:
      - name: model-storage
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: model-pvc

code_examples.description: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler configuration with custom metrics for AI inference queue depth
code_examples.code:
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: ai-learning-hpa
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: ai-learning-service
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80
  - type: Pods
    pods:
      metric:
        name: inference_queue_depth
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: "10"
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 10
        periodSeconds: 60
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 100
        periodSeconds: 15
      - type: Pods
        value: 4
        periodSeconds: 15
      selectPolicy: Max

code_examples.description: FastAPI inference server with async processing, batching, and caching
code_examples.code:
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List, Optional
import asyncio
import hashlib
import time
from functools import lru_cache

app = FastAPI(title="AI Learning Inference Server")

# Simple in-memory cache for demonstration
# In production, use Redis with semantic similarity matching
inference_cache = {}
request_queue = asyncio.Queue()

class InferenceRequest(BaseModel):
    student_id: str
    question: str
    context: Optional[List[str]] = []
    model_name: str = "default"

class InferenceResponse(BaseModel):
    answer: str
    confidence: float
    model_used: str
    cached: bool
    latency_ms: float

async def process_inference_batch(requests: List[InferenceRequest]) -> List[dict]:
    # Simulate batch inference processing
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # Actual model inference would happen here
    results = []
    for req in requests:
        cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{req.question}:{req.model_name}".encode()).hexdigest()
        if cache_key in inference_cache:
            cached_result = inference_cache[cache_key]
            cached_result["cached"] = True
            results.append(cached_result)
        else:
            result = {
                "answer": f"Processed response for: {req.question[:50]}",
                "confidence": 0.92,
                "model_used": req.model_name,
                "cached": False
            }
            inference_cache[cache_key] = result
            results.append(result)
    return results

@app.post("/v1/inference", response_model=InferenceResponse)
async def inference(request: InferenceRequest):
    start_time = time.time()
    
    cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{request.question}:{request.model_name}".encode()).hexdigest()
    
    if cache_key in inference_cache:
        result = inference_cache[cache_key].copy()
        result["cached"] = True
        result["latency_ms"] = (time.time() - start_time) * 1000
        return result
    
    # Add to queue for batch processing
    await request_queue.put(request)
    
    # Process in batch if queue is large enough
    batch = []
    if request_queue.qsize() >= 5:
        for _ in range(min(5, request_queue.qsize())):
            batch.append(await request_queue.get())
        results = await process_inference_batch(batch)
        for r in results:
            inference_cache[cache_key] = r
    else:
        # Process single request
        results = await process_inference_batch([request])
        result = results[0]
        inference_cache[cache_key] = result
    
    result["latency_ms"] = (time.time() - start_time) * 1000
    return result

@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {"status": "healthy", "queue_depth": request_queue.qsize()}

@app.get("/metrics")
async def metrics():
    return {
        "cache_size": len(inference_cache),
        "queue_depth": request_queue.qsize(),
        "cache_hit_rate": 0.75
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)

code_examples.description: Intelligent cost optimization layer for LLM API calls with model routing and caching
code_examples.code:
import hashlib
import time
from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Any
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
import asyncio

class ModelTier(Enum):
    FAST_BUDGET = "fast_budget"      # Small, fast, cheap models
    BALANCED = "balanced"            # Mid-tier models
    PREMIUM = "premium"             # High-capability, expensive models

@dataclass
class LLMConfig:
    model_name: str
    tier: ModelTier
    cost_per_1k_tokens: float
    avg_latency_ms: float
    max_tokens: int

class CostOptimizer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.model_configs = {
            "gpt-4": LLMConfig("gpt-4", ModelTier.PREMIUM, 0.03, 5000, 8192),
            "gpt-3.5-turbo": LLMConfig("gpt-3.5-turbo", ModelTier.BALANCED, 0.002, 1000, 4096),
            "llama-2-7b": LLMConfig("llama-2-7b", ModelTier.FAST_BUDGET, 0.0, 200, 4096),
        }
        self.response_cache: Dict[str, Any] = {}
        self.usage_stats = {model: {"requests": 0, "tokens": 0, "cost": 0.0} 
                            for model in self.model_configs.keys()}
        self.daily_budget = 100.0
        self.today_spend = 0.0
    
    def estimate_cost(self, model: str, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int) -> float:
        config = self.model_configs.get(model)
        if not config:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown model: {model}")
        total_tokens = prompt_tokens + completion_tokens
        return (total_tokens / 1000) * config.cost_per_1k_tokens
    
    def select_model(self, task_complexity: str, urgency: str) -> str:
        # Model selection logic based on task requirements
        if self.today_spend >= self.daily_budget:
            return "llama-2-7b"  # Fallback to free model
        
        if urgency == "high" or task_complexity == "high":
            return "gpt-4"
        elif task_complexity == "medium":
            return "gpt-3.5-turbo"
        else:
            return "llama-2-7b"
    
    def get_cache_key(self, prompt: str, model: str) -> str:
        return hashlib.sha256(f"{prompt}:{model}".encode()).hexdigest()
    
    async def cached_llm_call(
        self, 
        prompt: str, 
        task_complexity: str = "medium",
        urgency: str = "normal",
        use_cache: bool = True
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        # Select appropriate model
        model = self.select_model(task_complexity, urgency)
        cache_key = self.get_cache_key(prompt, model)
        
        # Check cache first
        if use_cache and cache_key in self.response_cache:
            return {
                "response": self.response_cache[cache_key],
                "model": model,
                "cached": True,
                "cost": 0.0
            }
        
        # Estimate tokens (in production, use proper tokenizer)
        prompt_tokens = len(prompt.split()) * 1.3  # Rough approximation
        
        # Simulate LLM API call
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # Actual API call would happen here
        
        response = f"Generated response from {model} for: {prompt[:50]}..."
        completion_tokens = len(response.split())
        
        # Calculate and track cost
        cost = self.estimate_cost(model, int(prompt_tokens), completion_tokens)
        self.today_spend += cost
        
        self.usage_stats[model]["requests"] += 1
        self.usage_stats[model]["tokens"] += int(prompt_tokens + completion_tokens)
        self.usage_stats[model]["cost"] += cost
        
        # Cache the response
        if use_cache:
            self.response_cache[cache_key] = response
        
        return {
            "response": response,
            "model": model,
            "cached": False,
            "cost": cost,
            "total_spend_today": self.today_spend
        }
    
    def get_optimization_report(self) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        total_cost = sum(stats["cost"] for stats in self.usage_stats.values())
        total_requests = sum(stats["requests"] for stats in self.usage_stats.values())
        cache_hits = len(self.response_cache)
        
        return {
            "daily_budget": self.daily_budget,
            "today_spend": self.today_spend,
            "remaining_budget": self.daily_budget - self.today_spend,
            "total_requests": total_requests,
            "cache_size": cache_hits,
            "model_breakdown": self.usage_stats,
            "avg_cost_per_request": total_cost / total_requests if total_requests > 0 else 0
        }

# Usage example
async def main():
    optimizer = CostOptimizer()
    
    # Simple question - uses fast/cheap model
    result1 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="What is the capital of France?",
        task_complexity="low"
    )
    print(f"Simple question: {result1['model']}, Cost: ${result1['cost']:.4f}")
    
    # Complex question - uses premium model
    result2 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="Explain quantum entanglement and its implications for cryptography",
        task_complexity="high",
        urgency="high"
    )
    print(f"Complex question: {result2['model']}, Cost: ${result2['cost']:.4f}")
    
    # Same simple question - should hit cache
    result3 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="What is the capital of France?",
        task_complexity="low"
    )
    print(f"Cached question: {result3['model']}, Cached: {result3['cached']}, Cost: ${result3['cost']:.4f}")
    
    print("\nOptimization Report:")
    print(optimizer.get_optimization_report())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

code_examples.description: Kubernetes Service with load balancing configuration for AI inference endpoints
code_examples.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: ai-learning-service-lb
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: ai-learning
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: ai-learning
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
    protocol: TCP
  - name: grpc
    port: 50051
    targetPort: 50051
    protocol: TCP
  sessionAffinity: ClientIP
  sessionAffinityConfig:
    clientIP:
      timeoutSeconds: 10800
  externalTrafficPolicy: Local
  healthCheckNodePort: 0
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: inference-config
  namespace: production
data:
  inference-config.yaml: |
    model_serving:
      default_model: "tutor-v2"
      timeout_seconds: 30
      max_retries: 3
      
    rate_limiting:
      requests_per_minute: 100
      burst: 20
      
    caching:
      enabled: true
      ttl_seconds: 3600
      max_entries: 10000
      
    load_balancing:
      algorithm: "weighted_least_request"
      health_check_interval: 10s
      
    cost_control:
      daily_budget_usd: 100.0
      fallback_model: "llama-2-7b"

code_examples.description: Deployment script for building Docker image, pushing to registry, and rolling update
code_examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
set -e

REGISTRY="your-registry.com"
IMAGE_NAME="ai-learning-service"
VERSION=${1:-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)}
NAMESPACE="production"

# Build the Docker image
echo "Building Docker image ${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION}..."
docker build -t ${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION} \
    --build-arg MODEL_VERSION=${VERSION} \
    --platform linux/amd64 \
    .

# Tag for registry
docker tag ${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION} ${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION}
docker tag ${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION} ${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:latest

# Push to registry
echo "Pushing images to registry..."
docker push ${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION}
docker push ${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:latest

# Update Kubernetes deployment
echo "Updating Kubernetes deployment..."
kubectl set image deployment/ai-learning-service \
    inference-server=${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION} \
    -n ${NAMESPACE}

# Monitor rollout
echo "Monitoring rollout..."
kubectl rollout status deployment/ai-learning-service \
    -n ${NAMESPACE} \
    --timeout=300s

# Verify the deployment
echo "Verifying deployment..."
kubectl get pods -n ${NAMESPACE} -l app=ai-learning

# Run basic health check
SERVICE_IP=$(kubectl get svc ai-learning-service-lb -n ${NAMESPACE} -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}')
curl -f http://${SERVICE_IP}/health || echo "Health check failed!"

echo "Deployment complete! Version: ${VERSION}"

code_examples.description: Multi-stage Dockerfile for AI inference service with model caching
code_examples.code:
# Stage 1: Build stage
FROM python:3.11-slim as builder

WORKDIR /app

# Install build dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    build-essential \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy requirements and install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --user -r requirements.txt

# Stage 2: Production stage
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install runtime dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    curl \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Create non-root user for security
RUN groupadd -r appgroup && useradd -r -g appgroup appuser

# Copy Python packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /root/.local /home/appuser/.local

# Copy application code
COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup . .

# Download or copy model files (in production, use volume mounts)
# RUN python -c "from transformers import AutoModel; AutoModel.from_pretrained('tutor-model')"

# Set environment variables
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PATH=/home/appuser/.local/bin:$PATH

# Switch to non-root user
USER appuser

# Expose ports
EXPOSE 8000 50051

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=40s --retries=3 \
    CMD curl -f http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1

# Run the application
CMD ["python", "-m", "uvicorn", "model_server:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

common_mistakes: Deploying models without resource limits, causing Kubernetes pods to consume unlimited memory and crash nodes
Using identical scaling policies for different model types; large language models require different thresholds than lightweight classifiers
Neglecting cold start latency in serverless or auto-scaled deployments; first requests after scale-out can timeout
Caching LLM responses without semantic similarity matching; exact-match caching misses opportunities where semantically similar inputs could share cached results
Failing to implement circuit breakers for external LLM APIs; a provider outage cascades to your entire application
Setting auto-scaling metrics based only on CPU/memory without considering inference queue depth, leading to poor responsiveness under load
Not implementing request timeouts for model inference, allowing slow requests to block resources indefinitely
Deploying without proper health checks for model readiness, routing traffic to pods still loading models
Ignoring request payload size limits; large context windows can quickly exhaust memory on inference servers
Skipping cost monitoring during development, leading to unexpected bills when production traffic patterns emerge
best_practices: Use multi-stage Docker builds to minimize image size and attack surface; production images should contain only runtime dependencies
Implement separate Kubernetes deployments for different model tiers (CPU inference, GPU inference) with appropriate resource requests
Configure rolling deployments with maxSurge=1 and maxUnavailable=0 for zero-downtime updates of AI services
Implement circuit breakers and fallback mechanisms for external API calls with graceful degradation to cached or simpler responses
Use Prometheus metrics and Grafana dashboards specifically for AI metrics: inference latency percentiles, queue depth, cache hit rates, and cost tracking
Enable request tracing (Jaeger, Zipkin) to identify bottlenecks across multi-model pipelines
Implement token budgeting and request validation at the API gateway level before requests reach expensive inference resources
Use model versioning with canary deployments: route 5-10% of traffic to new model versions before full rollout
Configure appropriate PodDisruptionBudgets to ensure minimum replicas remain available during node maintenance
Store model artifacts in shared PVCs or dedicated model registries (MLflow, Weights & Biases) rather than embedding in container images

practice.title: Deploy an AI Tutoring Service with Auto-Scaling and Cost Controls
practice.description: Create a complete production-ready deployment for an AI-powered tutoring service. Your deployment should include: (1) A containerized FastAPI application serving a learning assistant model, (2) Kubernetes manifests with auto-scaling configured for inference queue depth, (3) A caching layer using Redis for semantic caching, (4) Cost optimization logic that routes simple questions to a small fast model and complex questions to a larger model, (5) Load balancer configuration with health checks. Deploy to a local Kubernetes cluster (Minikube or Kind) or cloud Kubernetes service, then load test to verify auto-scaling behavior and cost tracking.
practice.expected_output: A Git repository containing: Kubernetes YAML manifests (deployment.yaml, service.yaml, hpa.yaml, configmap.yaml), a Dockerfile, the FastAPI application code with caching and cost optimization, a load test script demonstrating auto-scaling from 2 to 10+ replicas under load, and screenshots/metrics showing cache hit rates and cost savings compared to baseline deployment.

summary_points: Production deployment of AI systems requires containerization (Docker) and orchestration (Kubernetes) to achieve scalability, reliability, and resource efficiency
Model serving architectures must choose between real-time inference (interactive) and batch processing (periodic) based on application requirements
Caching strategies for AI applications extend beyond simple key-value stores to semantic caching that recognizes similar inputs
Cost optimization for LLM-based systems combines model routing, response caching, token budgeting, and usage monitoring to control API expenses
Load balancing and auto-scaling policies for AI workloads should consider inference-specific metrics like queue depth and latency rather than generic CPU/memory metrics alone

raw_json.title: Production Deployment Strategies
raw_json.description: Moving AI learning systems from development to production.
key_points: Containerized deployment with Kubernetes for scalability
Model serving infrastructure: batch and real-time inference
Load balancing and caching strategies for AI applications
Cost optimization for LLM API usage and model hosting
raw_json.detailed_content:
## Understanding Production Deployment for AI Systems

### Why Production Deployment Matters

When you develop an AI-assisted learning system in a notebook or local environment, you have full control over resources and can tolerate slow response times. Production deployment introduces new challenges: variable traffic patterns, the need for high availability, security requirements, monitoring, and cost management. A learning system that works perfectly for one user may fail catastrophically when serving 1,000 concurrent learners.

### Containerized Deployment with Kubernetes

Containerization packages your application along with all its dependencies, ensuring consistent behavior regardless of the deployment environment. Docker containers isolate your AI application from system-level conflicts and enable reproducible deployments.

Kubernetes, the industry standard for container orchestration, provides essential production capabilities:

**Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA)** automatically adjusts the number of container instances based on CPU utilization, memory usage, or custom metrics. For AI applications, you might scale based on request queue length or inference latency rather than simple CPU metrics.

**Rolling Updates and Rollbacks** allow you to deploy new model versions without downtime. Kubernetes gradually replaces old pods with new ones while maintaining service availability.

**Self-Healing** restarts failed containers, replaces unresponsive pods, and reschedules work to healthy nodes automatically.

**Namespaces and Resource Quotas** help you isolate different environments (development, staging, production) and prevent any single team or application from consuming all cluster resources.

### Model Serving Infrastructure

Model serving addresses how your trained models are exposed to applications. There are two primary paradigms:

**Real-Time Inference (Online Serving)** processes individual requests as they arrive, returning predictions within milliseconds to seconds. This approach suits interactive learning applications where students expect immediate feedback. Real-time serving typically uses REST or gRPC APIs, with models loaded into memory on specialized inference servers like TensorFlow Serving, TorchServe, or Triton Inference Server.

**Batch Inference (Offline Serving)** processes large volumes of requests accumulated over time. This approach suits periodic tasks like grading assignments overnight, generating personalized content bundles, or running analytics. Batch jobs can be scheduled with tools like Apache Airflow or Kubernetes CronJobs.

A sophisticated production system often implements both patterns: real-time inference for interactive features and batch processing for intensive computations that don't require immediate results.

### Load Balancing Strategies

Load balancers distribute incoming requests across multiple model instances, preventing any single instance from becoming overwhelmed. For AI applications, simple round-robin distribution may not be optimal because inference requests can have vastly different computational requirements.

**Intelligent Routing** considers current load, instance health, and request characteristics. For example, requests for simpler models might be routed differently than complex multi-model pipelines.

**Connection Pooling** maintains persistent connections to backend services, reducing the overhead of establishing new connections for each request.

**Health Checks** continuously verify that model instances are responsive and producing valid outputs, not just that they're running.

### Caching Strategies for AI Applications

Caching in AI systems differs from traditional web caching because identical inputs may not produce identical outputs (especially with stochastic models), and inference results can be computationally expensive to generate.

**Semantic Caching** stores inference results based on semantic similarity rather than exact input matching. For learning systems, this means caching responses for similar questions, allowing students who ask about the same concept to receive cached results.

**Prompt Caching** is particularly valuable for LLM-based applications. When a base prompt remains constant and only the student-specific input changes, you can cache the computed prompt embeddings and reuse them.

**Embedding Cache** stores vector representations of frequently accessed content (course materials, questions, previous student inputs), enabling fast similarity searches without recomputing embeddings.

### Cost Optimization for LLM and Model Hosting

LLM API costs can quickly dominate your operational budget. Effective cost optimization requires multiple strategies:

**Model Selection** matches model capability to task complexity. Not every request needs GPT-4 class capabilities; simpler tasks may work well with smaller, faster, cheaper models.

**Prompt Compression** reduces token count without losing essential information, directly reducing API costs.

**Response Caching** prevents redundant API calls for identical or semantically similar requests.

**Batch Processing** combines multiple requests into single API calls where the provider supports it, reducing per-request overhead.

**Self-Hosting Consideration** evaluates whether hosting open-source models on your infrastructure makes financial sense compared to API usage, considering your volume, latency requirements, and data privacy needs.

**Reserved Capacity** offers significant discounts compared to on-demand pricing for predictable workloads, such as core learning system features used during business hours.

## Code Examples

### Example 1

```text
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ai-learning-service
  labels:
    app: ai-learning
    environment: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ai-learning
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ai-learning
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: inference-server
```

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List, Optional
import asyncio
import hashlib
import time
from functools import lru_cache

app = FastAPI(title="AI Learning Inference Server")

# Simple in-memory cache for demonstration
# In production, use Redis with semantic similarity matching
inference_cache = {}
request_queue = asyncio.Queue()

class InferenceRequest(BaseModel):
    student_id: str
    question: str
    context: Optional[List[str]] = []
    model_name: str = "default"

class InferenceResponse(BaseModel):
    answer: str
    confidence: float
    model_used: str
    cached: bool
    latency_ms: float

async def process_inference_batch(requests: List[InferenceRequest]) -> List[dict]:
    # Simulate batch inference processing
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # Actual model inference would happen here
    results = []
    for req in requests:
        cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{req.question}:{req.model_name}".encode()).hexdigest()
        if cache_key in inference_cache:
            cached_result = inference_cache[cache_key]
            cached_result["cached"] = True
            results.append(cached_result)
        else:
            result = {
                "answer": f"Processed response for: {req.question[:50]}",
                "confidence": 0.92,
                "model_used": req.model_name,
                "cached": False
            }
            inference_cache[cache_key] = result
            results.append(result)
    return results

@app.post("/v1/inference", response_model=InferenceResponse)
async def inference(request: InferenceRequest):
    start_time = time.time()
    
    cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{request.question}:{request.model_name}".encode()).hexdigest()
    
    if cache_key in inference_cache:
        result = inference_cache[cache_key].copy()
        result["cached"] = True
        result["latency_ms"] = (time.time() - start_time) * 1000
        return result
    
    # Add to queue for batch processing
    await request_queue.put(request)
    
    # Process in batch if queue is large enough
    batch = []
    if request_queue.qsize() >= 5:
        for _ in range(min(5, request_queue.qsize())):
            batch.append(await request_queue.get())
        results = await process_inference_batch(batch)
        for r in results:
            inference_cache[cache_key] = r
    else:
        # Process single request
        results = await process_inference_batch([request])
        result = results[0]
        inference_cache[cache_key] = result
    
    result["latency_ms"] = (time.time() - start_time) * 1000
    return result

@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {"status": "healthy", "queue_depth": request_queue.qsize()}

@app.get("/metrics")
async def metrics():
    return {
        "cache_size": len(inference_cache),
        "queue_depth": request_queue.qsize(),
        "cache_hit_rate": 0.75
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
```

### Example 4

```python
import hashlib
import time
from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Any
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
import asyncio

class ModelTier(Enum):
    FAST_BUDGET = "fast_budget"      # Small, fast, cheap models
    BALANCED = "balanced"            # Mid-tier models
    PREMIUM = "premium"             # High-capability, expensive models

@dataclass
class LLMConfig:
    model_name: str
    tier: ModelTier
    cost_per_1k_tokens: float
    avg_latency_ms: float
    max_tokens: int

class CostOptimizer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.model_configs = {
            "gpt-4": LLMConfig("gpt-4", ModelTier.PREMIUM, 0.03, 5000, 8192),
            "gpt-3.5-turbo": LLMConfig("gpt-3.5-turbo", ModelTier.BALANCED, 0.002, 1000, 4096),
            "llama-2-7b": LLMConfig("llama-2-7b", ModelTier.FAST_BUDGET, 0.0, 200, 4096),
        }
        self.response_cache: Dict[str, Any] = {}
        self.usage_stats = {model: {"requests": 0, "tokens": 0, "cost": 0.0} 
                            for model in self.model_configs.keys()}
        self.daily_budget = 100.0
        self.today_spend = 0.0
    
    def estimate_cost(self, model: str, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int) -> float:
        config = self.model_configs.get(model)
        if not config:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown model: {model}")
        total_tokens = prompt_tokens + completion_tokens
        return (total_tokens / 1000) * config.cost_per_1k_tokens
    
    def select_model(self, task_complexity: str, urgency: str) -> str:
        # Model selection logic based on task requirements
        if self.today_spend >= self.daily_budget:
            return "llama-2-7b"  # Fallback to free model
        
        if urgency == "high" or task_complexity == "high":
            return "gpt-4"
        elif task_complexity == "medium":
            return "gpt-3.5-turbo"
        else:
            return "llama-2-7b"
    
    def get_cache_key(self, prompt: str, model: str) -> str:
        return hashlib.sha256(f"{prompt}:{model}".encode()).hexdigest()
    
    async def cached_llm_call(
        self, 
        prompt: str, 
        task_complexity: str = "medium",
        urgency: str = "normal",
        use_cache: bool = True
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        # Select appropriate model
        model = self.select_model(task_complexity, urgency)
        cache_key = self.get_cache_key(prompt, model)
        
        # Check cache first
        if use_cache and cache_key in self.response_cache:
            return {
                "response": self.response_cache[cache_key],
                "model": model,
                "cached": True,
                "cost": 0.0
            }
        
        # Estimate tokens (in production, use proper tokenizer)
        prompt_tokens = len(prompt.split()) * 1.3  # Rough approximation
        
        # Simulate LLM API call
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # Actual API call would happen here
        
        response = f"Generated response from {model} for: {prompt[:50]}..."
        completion_tokens = len(response.split())
        
        # Calculate and track cost
        cost = self.estimate_cost(model, int(prompt_tokens), completion_tokens)
        self.today_spend += cost
        
        self.usage_stats[model]["requests"] += 1
        self.usage_stats[model]["tokens"] += int(prompt_tokens + completion_tokens)
        self.usage_stats[model]["cost"] += cost
        
        # Cache the response
        if use_cache:
            self.response_cache[cache_key] = response
        
        return {
            "response": response,
            "model": model,
            "cached": False,
            "cost": cost,
            "total_spend_today": self.today_spend
        }
    
    def get_optimization_report(self) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        total_cost = sum(stats["cost"] for stats in self.usage_stats.values())
        total_requests = sum(stats["requests"] for stats in self.usage_stats.values())
        cache_hits = len(self.response_cache)
        
        return {
            "daily_budget": self.daily_budget,
            "today_spend": self.today_spend,
            "remaining_budget": self.daily_budget - self.today_spend,
            "total_requests": total_requests,
            "cache_size": cache_hits,
            "model_breakdown": self.usage_stats,
            "avg_cost_per_request": total_cost / total_requests if total_requests > 0 else 0
        }

# Usage example
async def main():
    optimizer = CostOptimizer()
    
    # Simple question - uses fast/cheap model
    result1 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="What is the capital of France?",
        task_complexity="low"
    )
    print(f"Simple question: {result1['model']}, Cost: ${result1['cost']:.4f}")
    
    # Complex question - uses premium model
    result2 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="Explain quantum entanglement and its implications for cryptography",
        task_complexity="high",
        urgency="high"
    )
    print(f"Complex question: {result2['model']}, Cost: ${result2['cost']:.4f}")
    
    # Same simple question - should hit cache
    result3 = await optimizer.cached_llm_call(
        prompt="What is the capital of France?",
        task_complexity="low"
    )
    print(f"Cached question: {result3['model']}, Cached: {result3['cached']}, Cost: ${result3['cost']:.4f}")
    
    print("\nOptimization Report:")
    print(optimizer.get_optimization_report())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -e

REGISTRY="your-registry.com"
IMAGE_NAME="ai-learning-service"
VERSION=${1:-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)}
NAMESPACE="production"

# Build the Docker image
echo "Building Docker image ${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION}..."
docker build -t ${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION} \
    --build-arg MODEL_VERSION=${VERSION} \
    --platform linux/amd64 \
    .

# Tag for registry
docker tag ${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION} ${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION}
docker tag ${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION} ${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:latest

# Push to registry
echo "Pushing images to registry..."
docker push ${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION}
docker push ${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:latest

# Update Kubernetes deployment
echo "Updating Kubernetes deployment..."
kubectl set image deployment/ai-learning-service \
    inference-server=${REGISTRY}/${IMAGE_NAME}:${VERSION} \
    -n ${NAMESPACE}

# Monitor rollout
echo "Monitoring rollout..."
kubectl rollout status deployment/ai-learning-service \
    -n ${NAMESPACE} \
    --timeout=300s

# Verify the deployment
echo "Verifying deployment..."
kubectl get pods -n ${NAMESPACE} -l app=ai-learning

# Run basic health check
SERVICE_IP=$(kubectl get svc ai-learning-service-lb -n ${NAMESPACE} -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}')
curl -f http://${SERVICE_IP}/health || echo "Health check failed!"

echo "Deployment complete! Version: ${VERSION}"
```

```sql
# Stage 1: Build stage
FROM python:3.11-slim as builder

WORKDIR /app

# Install build dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    build-essential \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy requirements and install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --user -r requirements.txt

# Stage 2: Production stage
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install runtime dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    curl \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Create non-root user for security
RUN groupadd -r appgroup && useradd -r -g appgroup appuser

# Copy Python packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /root/.local /home/appuser/.local

# Copy application code
COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup . .

# Download or copy model files (in production, use volume mounts)
# RUN python -c "from transformers import AutoModel; AutoModel.from_pretrained('tutor-model')"

# Set environment variables
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PATH=/home/appuser/.local/bin:$PATH

# Switch to non-root user
USER appuser

# Expose ports
EXPOSE 8000 50051

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=40s --retries=3 \
    CMD curl -f http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1

# Run the application
CMD ["python", "-m", "uvicorn", "model_server:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
```

## Common Mistakes

- Deploying models without resource limits, causing Kubernetes pods to consume unlimited memory and crash nodes
- Using identical scaling policies for different model types; large language models require different thresholds than lightweight classifiers
- Neglecting cold start latency in serverless or auto-scaled deployments; first requests after scale-out can timeout
- Caching LLM responses without semantic similarity matching; exact-match caching misses opportunities where semantically similar inputs could share cached results
- Failing to implement circuit breakers for external LLM APIs; a provider outage cascades to your entire application
- Setting auto-scaling metrics based only on CPU/memory without considering inference queue depth, leading to poor responsiveness under load
- Not implementing request timeouts for model inference, allowing slow requests to block resources indefinitely
- Deploying without proper health checks for model readiness, routing traffic to pods still loading models
- Ignoring request payload size limits; large context windows can quickly exhaust memory on inference servers
- Skipping cost monitoring during development, leading to unexpected bills when production traffic patterns emerge


## Best Practices

- Use multi-stage Docker builds to minimize image size and attack surface; production images should contain only runtime dependencies
- Implement separate Kubernetes deployments for different model tiers (CPU inference, GPU inference) with appropriate resource requests
- Configure rolling deployments with maxSurge=1 and maxUnavailable=0 for zero-downtime updates of AI services
- Implement circuit breakers and fallback mechanisms for external API calls with graceful degradation to cached or simpler responses
- Use Prometheus metrics and Grafana dashboards specifically for AI metrics: inference latency percentiles, queue depth, cache hit rates, and cost tracking
- Enable request tracing (Jaeger, Zipkin) to identify bottlenecks across multi-model pipelines
- Implement token budgeting and request validation at the API gateway level before requests reach expensive inference resources
- Use model versioning with canary deployments: route 5-10% of traffic to new model versions before full rollout
- Configure appropriate PodDisruptionBudgets to ensure minimum replicas remain available during node maintenance
- Store model artifacts in shared PVCs or dedicated model registries (MLflow, Weights & Biases) rather than embedding in container images
